title: Fragment Of An ï؟½Antigoneï؟½
content:
آ آ آ آ THE CHORUS

آ آ آ آ Well hath he done who hath seizï؟½d happiness.
آ آ آ آ For little do the all-containing Hours,
آ آ آ آ Though opulent, freely give.
آ آ آ آ Who, weighing that life well
آ آ آ آ Fortune presents unprayï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Declines her ministry, and carves his own:
آ آ آ آ And, justice not infringï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Makes his own welfare his unswervï؟½d-from law.

آ آ آ آ He does well too, who keeps that clue the mild
آ آ آ آ Birth-Goddess and the austere Fates first gave.
آ آ آ آ For from the clay when these
آ آ آ آ Bring him, a weeping child,
آ آ آ آ First to the light, and mark
آ آ آ آ A country for him, kinsfolk, and a home,
آ آ آ آ Unguided he remains,
آ آ آ آ Till the Fates come again, alone, with death.

آ آ آ آ In little companies,
آ آ آ آ And, our own place once left,
آ آ آ آ Ignorant where to stand, or whom to avoid,
آ آ آ آ By city and household groupï؟½d, we live: and many shocks
آ آ آ آ Our order heaven-ordainï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Must every day endure.
آ آ آ آ Voyages, exiles, hates, dissensions, wars.
آ آ آ آ Besides what waste He makes,
آ آ آ آ The all-hated, order-breaking.
آ آ آ آ Without friend, city, or home,
آ آ آ آ Death, who dissevers all.

آ آ آ آ Him then I praise, who dares
آ آ آ آ To self-selected good
آ آ آ آ Prefer obedience to the primal law,
آ آ آ آ Which consecrates the ties of blood: for these, indeed,
آ آ آ آ Are to the Gods a care:
آ آ آ آ That touches but himself.
آ آ آ آ For every day man may be linkï؟½d and loos ï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With strangers: but the bond
آ آ آ آ Original, deep-inwound,
آ آ آ آ Of blood, can he not bind
آ آ آ آ Nor, if Fate binds, not bear.

آ آ آ آ But hush! Haemon, whom Antigone,
آ آ آ آ Robbing herself of life in burying,
آ آ آ آ Against Creonï؟½s law, Polynices,
آ آ آ آ Robs of a lovï؟½d bride; pale, imploring,
آ آ آ آ Waiting her passage,
آ آ آ آ Forth from the palace hitherward comes.

آ آ آ آ HAEMON

آ آ آ آ No, no, old men, Creon I curse not.
آ آ آ آ I weep, Thebans,
آ آ آ آ One than Creon crueller far.
آ آ آ آ For he, he, at least, by slaying her,
آ آ آ آ August laws doth mightily vindicate:
آ آ آ آ But thou, too-bold, headstrong, pitiless,
آ آ آ آ Ah me!, honourest more than thy lover,
آ آ آ آ O Antigone,
آ آ آ آ A dead, ignorant, thankless corpse.

آ آ آ آ THE CHORUS

آ آ آ آ Nor was the love untrue
آ آ آ آ Which the Dawn-Goddess bore
آ آ آ آ To that fair youth she erst
آ آ آ آ Leaving the salt sea-beds
آ آ آ آ And coming flushï؟½d over the stormy frith
آ آ آ آ Of loud Euripus, saw
آ آ آ آ Saw and snatchï؟½d, wild with love,
آ آ آ آ From the pine-dotted spurs
آ آ آ آ Of Parnes, where thy waves,
آ آ آ آ Asopus, gleam rock-hemmï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ The Hunter of the Tanagraean Field.
آ آ آ آ But him, in his sweet prime,
آ آ آ آ By severance immature,
آ آ آ آ By Artemisï؟½ soft shafts,
آ آ آ آ She, though a Goddess born,
آ آ آ آ Saw in the rocky isle of Delos die.
آ آ آ آ Such end oï؟½ertook that love.
آ آ آ آ For she desirï؟½d to make
آ آ آ آ Immortal mortal man,
آ آ آ آ And blend his happy life,
آ آ آ آ Far from the Gods, with hers:
آ آ آ آ To him postponing an eternal law.

آ آ آ آ HAEMON

آ آ آ آ But, like me, she, wroth, complaining,
آ آ آ آ Succumbï؟½d to the envy of unkind Gods:
آ آ آ آ And, her beautiful arms unclasping,
آ آ آ آ Her fair Youth unwillingly gave.

آ آ آ آ THE CHORUS

آ آ آ آ Nor, though enthronï؟½d too high
آ آ آ آ To fear assault of envious Gods,
آ آ آ آ His belovï؟½d Argive Seer would Zeus retain
آ آ آ آ From his appointed end
آ آ آ آ In this our Thebes: but when

آ آ آ آ His flying steeds came near
آ آ آ آ To cross the steep Ismenian glen,
آ آ آ آ The broad Earth openï؟½d and whelmï؟½d them and him;
آ آ آ آ And through the void air sang
آ آ آ آ At large his enemyï؟½s spear.

آ آ آ آ And fain would Zeus have savï؟½d his tired son
آ آ آ آ Beholding him where the Two Pillars stand
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er the sun-reddenï؟½d Western Straits:
آ آ آ آ Or at his work in that dim lower world.
آ آ آ آ Fain would he have recallï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The fraudulent oath which bound
آ آ آ آ To a much feebler wight the heroic man:

آ آ آ آ But he preferrï؟½d Fate to his strong desire.
آ آ آ آ Nor did there need less than the burning pile
آ آ آ آ Under the towering Trachis crags,
آ آ آ آ And the Spercheiusï؟½ vale, shaken with groans,
آ آ آ آ And the rousï؟½d Maliac gulph,
آ آ آ آ And scarï؟½d Oetaean snows,
آ آ آ آ To achieve his sonï؟½s deliverance, O my child.
meaning:
About: Matthew Arnold (24 December 1822 ï؟½ 15 April 1888) was an English poet and cultural critic, who worked as an inspector of schools. 
He was the son of Thomas Arnold, the famed headmaster of Rugby School, and brother to Tom Arnold, literary professor, and William Delafield Arnold, novelist and colonial administrator.
